articles.title: Extreme heat: Apathy adds to suffering of marginalised communities - Deccan Herald
articles.full_text:
Bengaluru: The launch of Heat Collective, a platform of 11 organisations coming together to tackle heat wave challenges, brought to the fore the challenges faced by construction labourers, domestic workers, Asha workers and waste pickers.
Usha Rani, Asha worker in the Weaver Colony at Bengaluru south's Gottigere, noted that as the first point of contact that informs the government about the health of highly vulnerable population, she puts her own health at risk while doing the job. "I leave house at 10 am and come back at 7 pm. Throughout the day, I avoid drinking water as much as possible because I don't have access to toilet," she said.
Pushpa , a domestic worker from Ragigudda, said some of her employers do not allow her to use the toilet. "If I take a day's leave due to fever, they keep the previous day's utensils without washing. However, we can't use their toilets. Some do not even give water," she said.
Construction worker Mohammed Ibrahim said he came from Lingasugur in Raichur, said even after the sun sets, heat in his shed doesn't go down. "I go to sleep after working in the sun throughout the day. However, the heat in the shed hardly allows me to sleep. We don't have a window and can't open the doors if we have to save any valuables. We sleep about one or two hours and rush to work the next morning. I have land in my home town which I cannot cultivate. Working in this extreme heat is inevitable to repay the money we have borrowed," he added.
Indira, a waste picker from cement colony, JC Road, said along with the dust and heat, she faces discrimination. "I didn't want my children to face the same problem and ensured that all four of them get education. Working in the city has become difficult due to rising heat in the last two years. In addition, many people are cutting trees for aesthetics. We don't even have some shade to rest. There is no clean water to drink, after we complete the work. The government can provide drinking water at bus stops," she said
